policies.name: Obvious pricing-error order honouring decision
policies.description: Orders captured at an obviously wrong price (the S$3,854 laser printer listed at S$66 in Digilandmall) raise the unilateral-mistake question: honour, cancel, or offer goodwill compensation. That is a combined legal and commercial judgement reserved for a human, not a customer-service automation.
regulatory_references: Industry best practice — unilateral mistake doctrine, Chwee Kin Keong v Digilandmall.com [2005] SGCA 2

policies.name: Flash-sale price override sign-off
policies.description: Manual price overrides pushed mid-flash-sale bypass the merchandising guardrails that prevent loss-making or discriminatory prices going live to thousands of shoppers at once. A merchandiser confirms the override before it publishes.
regulatory_references: Industry best practice — dual control on live promotional price changes

policies.name: Bulk discount code generation approval
policies.description: Mass-generated promo codes are a classic margin-leak and resale-fraud vector: a single unlimited-use code posted to a coupon forum can erase a quarter's promotion budget overnight. Promotions desk approves code count, usage caps and expiry before issuance.
regulatory_references: Industry best practice — promotion abuse and coupon-fraud controls

policies.name: Drip pricing in checkout copy
policies.description: Checkout or product-page copy that reveals mandatory fees only at the final payment step is drip pricing — unlawful single-price conduct in Australia and a named dark pattern in India. Compliance reviews the copy so the headline price states the full minimum payable amount.
regulatory_references: Australian Consumer Law s.48 — single price disclosure
India Guidelines for Prevention and Regulation of Dark Patterns 2023 — drip pricing

message_contains_any: add the service fee at the last step

policies.name: Fictitious was/now savings claims
policies.description: Strikethrough comparisons against a 'before' price the product never genuinely sold at are false trade descriptions in Hong Kong and misleading price representations in Japan. Any agent-drafted markdown copy citing prior prices is verified against actual sales history before publication.
regulatory_references: Hong Kong Trade Descriptions Ordinance (Cap. 362) s.7 — false trade descriptions
Japan Act against Unjustifiable Premiums and Misleading Representations art. 5(2) — misleading price representations

message_contains_any: never sold at the higher price

policies.name: Cross-border GST and duties disclosure
policies.description: Cross-border listings that understate or omit GST, import duties or customs charges leave shoppers with surprise collect-on-delivery bills and expose the seller to single-price breaches. Tax-disclosure wording in storefront or checkout copy gets a human check before going live.
regulatory_references: Australian Consumer Law s.48 — single price must include taxes and quantifiable charges

policies.name: Chargeback representment handling
policies.description: Assembling a representment package commits the merchant to a contested position with the card schemes: weak compelling evidence wastes the fee, while disputing a genuine fraud victim invites regulator attention. A disputes analyst owns every representment and pre-arbitration decision.
regulatory_references: Industry best practice — card scheme dispute resolution rules (Visa CE3.0 / Mastercard chargeback guide)

policies.name: Serial-returner account intervention
policies.description: Wardrobing, bracketing and habitual-returner patterns justify intervention, but blocking returns wholesale risks denying statutory remedies for genuinely defective goods — consumer guarantees cannot be contracted away. A human weighs the abuse evidence before any account restriction is applied.
regulatory_references: Australian Consumer Law ss.54–59 — consumer guarantees cannot be excluded

policies.name: Refund-without-return above threshold
policies.description: Keep-the-item refunds are economical for low-value goods but become a repeat-abuse magnet once amounts climb. Above the threshold a supervisor confirms the item genuinely is not worth recovering before the agent waives the return.

message_contains_any: no need to send it back

policies.name: Bulk-order refund escalation
policies.description: Refunds at wholesale or bulk-order scale carry inventory write-back, restocking-fee and relationship consequences a frontline automation cannot weigh. A disputes specialist negotiates the remedy mix (refund, replacement, credit) directly with the buyer.

policies.name: Warranty claim denial review
policies.description: Denying a warranty claim can extinguish remedies the law guarantees regardless of the manufacturer's warranty card — acceptable quality under the ACL, lemon-law remedies under the CPFTA. Denials are never automated; a consumer-guarantees specialist reviews the defect evidence and the statutory position first.
regulatory_references: Australian Consumer Law ss.54–55 — acceptable quality and fitness for purpose
Singapore CPFTA Part III (ss.12A–12F) — lemon law remedies

policies.name: First-party misuse pattern flag
policies.description: Item-not-received and empty-box claims that fit a first-party misuse pattern are still resolved normally — most are honest — but the case is flagged so repeat behaviour accumulates a visible risk history for the fraud team.

policies.name: Return label reissue automation
policies.description: Reprinting or re-emailing a return shipping label for an already-authorised return moves no money and changes no entitlement — the canonical safe automation in returns handling.

policies.name: Counterfeit listing takedown
policies.description: Counterfeit reports trigger takedown of a live listing — supplying forged-trademark goods is a criminal offence in Hong Kong — but a wrongful takedown destroys a legitimate seller's revenue and invites counter-claims. Brand protection validates the infringement evidence before the listing is pulled.
regulatory_references: Hong Kong Trade Descriptions Ordinance (Cap. 362) s.9 — forged trade marks

policies.name: Marketplace seller payout hold
policies.description: Freezing a seller's settlement is the marketplace equivalent of freezing a bank account: justified for live fraud, ruinous if mistaken, and increasingly scrutinised under platform-fairness rules. A marketplace risk officer owns every payout hold and its documented rationale.
regulatory_references: Industry best practice — marketplace settlement and platform-fairness governance

policies.name: Gift card bulk issuance interdiction
policies.description: Bulk gift-card issuance is the favourite cash-out rail for account-takeover and social-engineering fraud because codes are instantly liquid and untraceable once redeemed. Any bulk generation or mass value load goes to the fraud team for live handling, never automated fulfilment.
regulatory_references: Industry best practice — stored-value fraud and cash-out controls

message_contains_any: load value onto multiple gift cards

policies.name: Undisclosed paid placement in search ranking
policies.description: Boosting a listing's search position for payment without a 'sponsored' label is stealth marketing under Japan's 2023 designation and a disguised advertisement under India's dark-pattern guidelines. Ranking changes tied to commercial consideration require a compliance check on the disclosure.
regulatory_references: Japan Act against Unjustifiable Premiums and Misleading Representations — stealth marketing designation (effective Oct 2023)
India Guidelines for Prevention and Regulation of Dark Patterns 2023 — disguised advertisement

message_contains_any: hide that it is an ad

policies.name: Cloned brand storefront response
policies.description: A storefront cloning an official brand shop deceives shoppers about sponsorship and affiliation — a named unfair practice under the CPFTA — and usually signals a wider fraud operation. Brand protection takes over directly to coordinate takedown, evidence preservation and buyer remediation.
regulatory_references: Singapore CPFTA Second Schedule — false claims of sponsorship, approval or affiliation

message_contains_any: pretending to be the authorised dealer

policies.name: Verified seller routine listing edits
policies.description: Title, description and image refreshes from sellers who have passed marketplace verification are routine catalogue hygiene; gating them would throttle thousands of harmless daily edits. They auto-publish, with category and claims linting still applied downstream.

policies.name: Fake review ring removal
policies.description: Removing reviews that detection has attributed to bot networks, review farms or coordinated five-star campaigns is integrity-positive and time-sensitive, so the agent may act immediately. The removal is flagged into the risk record so affected sellers can contest it through the appeals queue.
regulatory_references: Australian Consumer Law s.18 — misleading or deceptive conduct

policies.name: Incentivised positive review interdiction
policies.description: Offering discounts, cash or free product specifically in exchange for positive ratings manufactures misleading endorsements — squarely within ACL s.18 and Japan's stealth-marketing prohibition. The agent refuses to draft or send such offers outright; there is no legitimate variant to escalate.
regulatory_references: Australian Consumer Law s.18 — misleading or deceptive conduct
Japan Act against Unjustifiable Premiums and Misleading Representations — stealth marketing designation (effective Oct 2023)

message_contains_any: discount for a five star review

policies.name: Subscription cancellation friction interdiction
policies.description: Requests to hide cancel buttons, add retention hoops or otherwise obstruct subscription cancellation are the 'subscription trap' dark pattern — prohibited in India and a prohibited act under Korea's e-commerce law. The design request is blocked; the customer's underlying right to cancel is untouched (see rec_routine_subscription_cancellation_policy).
regulatory_references: India Guidelines for Prevention and Regulation of Dark Patterns 2023 — subscription trap
Korea Act on the Consumer Protection in Electronic Commerce art. 21 — prohibited acts

policies.name: Routine subscription cancellation automation
policies.description: A shopper asking to cancel a subscription or switch off auto-renewal exercises a protected withdrawal right; processing it instantly is exactly the frictionless path regulators expect. The agent completes the cancellation without any retention gate.
regulatory_references: Korea Act on the Consumer Protection in Electronic Commerce art. 17 — right of withdrawal

policies.name: False urgency and scarcity copy review
policies.description: Countdown timers that reset and 'only a few left' banners on well-stocked SKUs are the false-urgency dark pattern in India and a false limited-availability claim under the CPFTA. Urgency and scarcity copy must be backed by real inventory or deadline data before a human approves publication.
regulatory_references: India Guidelines for Prevention and Regulation of Dark Patterns 2023 — false urgency
Singapore CPFTA Second Schedule — false limited-availability claims

message_contains_any: only a few left when stock is plentiful

policies.name: Environmental marketing claims substantiation
policies.description: Carbon-neutral, biodegradable and recyclability claims are a live greenwashing enforcement priority and are misleading conduct unless substantiated for the product's full lifecycle. Sustainability copy is held for review against the supplier's evidence file.
regulatory_references: Australian Consumer Law s.18 — misleading or deceptive conduct

policies.name: Influencer ad-disclosure omission review
policies.description: Briefing influencers to skip the ad label turns paid endorsement into stealth marketing, which Japan brought inside the Premiums Act in October 2023. Campaign briefs that touch disclosure wording route to marketing compliance before they reach the creator.
regulatory_references: Japan Act against Unjustifiable Premiums and Misleading Representations — stealth marketing designation (effective Oct 2023)

message_contains_any: do not mark it as sponsored

policies.name: Product recall communication sign-off
policies.description: Recall notices carry statutory urgency — Australia requires recall notification to the Commonwealth Minister within two days, and India's CCPA holds direct recall powers — so wording, scope and channel mix must be right the first time. The agent drafts at full speed, but a named human approves before anything is sent; this duty can never be switched off by a tenant.
regulatory_references: Australian Consumer Law s.122 — recall notification to the Commonwealth Minister
India Consumer Protection Act 2019 s.20 — CCPA recall powers

policies.name: Product safety injury complaint takeover
policies.description: Complaints describing fire, electric shock, choking hazards or injury are potential mandatory-report events — Australia gives suppliers two days to report serious injury or death — and each one may be the first signal of a defect cluster. The product safety office takes the conversation over live.
regulatory_references: Australian Consumer Law s.131 — mandatory reporting of serious injury, illness or death

policies.name: Age-restricted goods to minors interdiction
policies.description: Completing or facilitating a sale of alcohol, blades or other age-restricted goods to a known or suspected minor, or bypassing age verification to do so, is a statutory offence with no remediation path. The action is blocked unconditionally and the interdiction cannot be disabled by any tenant.
regulatory_references: Singapore Liquor Control (Supply and Consumption) Act 2015 — supply of liquor to minors
Industry best practice — age verification for restricted goods

policies.name: Recalled or banned product relisting interdiction
policies.description: Putting recalled batches or banned goods back on sale defeats the entire recall mechanism and is an offence under the ACL's product safety regime. Relisting requests are refused outright, and this interdiction is not tenant-tunable.
regulatory_references: Australian Consumer Law Pt 3-3 — supply of banned or recalled consumer goods

policies.name: Safety documentation request automation
policies.description: Sending a shopper the safety data sheet, instruction manual or compliance certificate for a product they bought is pure information provision that improves safe use — fully automatable.

policies.name: Loyalty point bulk adjustment approval
policies.description: Mass point credits are real money in deferred form: a mis-scoped goodwill top-up across all accounts creates an instant redemption liability and an arbitrage window for point brokers. Loyalty operations approves the segment, amount and expiry before the adjustment runs.
regulatory_references: Industry best practice — stored-value liability controls for loyalty programmes

message_contains_any: goodwill points top up for everyone

policies.name: Shopper account takeover indicators
policies.description: The classic takeover sequence — credential change, then a new device, then an address swap and a rush order — must be interrupted by a person who can verify the real account owner out-of-band before goods or stored value leave the account. Automated handling here is what the fraudster is counting on.
regulatory_references: Industry best practice — account takeover response playbooks

policies.name: Post-dispatch delivery address change
policies.description: Rerouting a parcel after dispatch is a favourite interception move in account-takeover and triangulation fraud, and it also voids most carrier liability. A human confirms the requester controls the original order before any redirect is filed with the carrier.

message_contains_any: divert the package to a new address

policies.name: Vulnerable consumer transaction takeover
policies.description: Signals that a sale exploited an elderly or cognitively impaired shopper, or used pressure tactics, engage the CPFTA's unfair-practice provisions and the ACL's unconscionable conduct standard. A trained advocate handles the case personally — unwinding the sale where warranted — rather than an automation applying standard returns logic.
regulatory_references: Singapore CPFTA s.4(c) — taking advantage of a consumer unable to protect their interests
Australian Consumer Law s.21 — unconscionable conduct

message_contains_any: did not understand what they signed up for

policies.name: Routine loyalty redemption automation
policies.description: Balance enquiries and applying a shopper's own points or reward vouchers at checkout are the bread-and-butter of loyalty servicing — low value, fully reversible, and safe to automate end to end.

message_contains_any: use my loyalty points at checkout

policies.name: High-value first order on new account flag
policies.description: A brand-new or guest account opening with an unusually large basket is the highest-yield card-testing and stolen-card profile in retail fraud models. The order proceeds — most are legitimate gift or festival purchases — but the elevated risk score routes it into enhanced post-order screening.
